steps.text: Initiate and develop proposed NPIs within the scope of authority or significantly affecting the operations of programs.
Request review and clearance by appropriate directorates, regional offices, Office of the Solicitor (SOL), the Assistant Secretary, etc., based on the subject and intended application.
Provide proposed NPI and necessary background information to ensure the proposed issuance is thoroughly understood and considered.
Establish a due date for response return.
Review and consider comments received and address them as appropriate.
Refer to the appropriate Deputy Assistant Secretary any unsolved disagreements or issues concerning the substance, potential policy implications, or priority of the proposed NPI.

Ensure that systems for receiving, reviewing, tracking, concurring, and issuing NPIs are effective for consistently complying with requirements (e.g., electronic tracking, paperless sign-off grids, and e-folders).
Consider each proposed NPI (and associated issues) and promptly respond to the originator regarding technical considerations, potential impact, Agency-wide implications, and anticipated reception.
Initiate and develop proposed policy issuances.
Consult with the Office of the Solicitor or Regional Office of the Solicitor if there is any doubt regarding whether they should review and clear an NPI.
Specify the official authorized to approve the release of the NPI.
Specify the official responsible for maintaining records documenting the views and clearance of each clearance official.
Specify the official responsible for maintaining and updating these written procedures.
Provide the Director, Directorate of Administrative Programs, a copy of the procedure and any future amendments.
Submit NPIs to the web administrator.
Submit NPI to the web administrator for publication.
Routinely review published NPIs to ensure information accurately reflects the Agency’s current policies and procedures.
Update or archive outdated NPIs as appropriate.
Clear national directives implementing OMB Circulars with the appropriate offices of OMB.
Clear directives affecting other federal agencies' programs with those agencies.
Communicate frequently with Agency offices and stakeholders to agree on policies, procedures, and required actions during the initial drafting process.
Informally circulate drafts, reports, and other materials relating to a directive drafting before conducting the formal clearance process.
Consult with DEP and DCSP for federal program change language during the informal clearance.
Submit construction-related directives to DOC for review.
Establish regional clearance procedures for regional directives (for Regional Offices).
Schedule a meeting with the appropriate Deputy Assistant Secretary to resolve the disagreement on comments.
Elevate outstanding issues to the Assistant Secretary for a decision.
Provide union notification in accordance with Local 12 and NCFLL Collective Bargaining Agreements.
Ensure all final unsigned directives are 508 compliant.
Submit final unsigned directives to the directives officer.
Submit publication requests to the web administrator.
Use the appropriate directive template to meet directive requirements.
Tentatively identify the type of directive and assign a subject following consultation with the directives officer.
Number the regional directive or notice.
Update the directives list on the OSHA Directives webpage.
Review non-substantive changes.
Implement and comply with the provisions of this instruction and with related instructions governing the management of policy and non-policy issues.
Self-assess and routinely monitor the directive process using the checklist on the directives tools webpage to ensure compliance with the provisions of the instruction.
Review and assess active directives on an annual basis with a final document of the review findings maintained in the national office or regional files.
Delegate the authority of review and assessment, as well as responsibility for preparing a final document of the review findings, as appropriate.
Conduct a full assessment of the files for the directives approved that year and ensure that all appropriate documents are included in the file.
Send an email or memo to the Director, Office of Audit, DAP, or designee documenting the completion of the requirement.
Provide documentation of completion to the directives officer.
Retain completion documentation in the directive file record.
Confirm the self-assessment documentation was received within the established deadline in the first quarter of each fiscal year.
Include the auditing of the assessment process as part of audit responsibilities and ensure that the directives process is part of regular auditing tasks.
Submit webpage updates to the directives officer.
Review the OSHA Directives webpage biennially for accuracy.
Archive directives containing an expiration date automatically.
Provide notification to national office directors and regional administrators of expiring directives for the upcoming fiscal year in Q4 of the current fiscal year.
Archive directives that are canceled by reference in the cancellation paragraph of a new or revised instruction, notice, or direction upon approval of the request.
Keep an OSHA Notice for the purpose of directive cancellation on the active publications page for 90 days.
Archive the cancellation notice at the end of 90 days.

Include the banner "NOTICE: This is an OSHA Archive Document and may no longer represent OSHA Policy. It is presented here as historical content, for research and review purposes only. Return to current directives." on all archived documents.

preconditions.text: All NPIs must be 508 compliant before submission for publication.
The national directive implements OMB Circulars.
The directive affects other federal agencies' programs.
There is a failure to achieve an agreement on comments from clearance offices.
There is an absence of resolution of the issue(s) from the Deputy Assistant Secretary.
Confirm the current directive template is being used prior to clearance review.
If the regional directive supplements a national directive
If a regional notice is issued for cancellation of an instruction
If changes are non-substantive (correcting typographical errors, grammar, syntax, minor factual inaccuracies, or updating outdated references)
Webpage updates must be submitted to the directives officer, as requests submitted directly to the web administrator will be returned.

constraints.text: Allow no more than three weeks turnaround time for response return.
Files must be maintained in accordance with OSHA file retention policies.
Written procedures must adhere to established Agency policy and procedure regarding such issuances, and to the provisions in this directive.
Written procedures must be consistent with all other statements of Agency policy and procedure.
Written procedures must be necessary to the effective management of Agency policies, procedures, and programs.
Written procedures must explicitly reference the governing policy or procedures, at a minimum through reference to the CFR, FR, or OSHA Directives number and effective date.
Written procedures must be maintained in files searchable by subject and date.
Written procedures must include a standardized disclaimer statement, approved by the Assistant Secretary with advice from the SOL, appropriately highlighted.
The originating Program Director/Regional Administrator will not contact the union directly.
Communication must be between the Office of Employee and Labor Management Relations and the Office of Audit.
Use the appropriate directive template to meet directive requirements.
Revisions of directives must retain the original sequential number and indicate the most recent effective date.
Only the most recent issuance must be included on the directives list.
Review must be conducted by the Program Director (Directors of Directorates and Free-Standing Offices) or Regional Administrator of the office.
No later than September 30 of the fiscal year

All archived documents will include the following banner: 'NOTICE: This is an OSHA Archive Document and may no longer represent OSHA Policy. It is presented here as historical content, for research and review purposes...'
